threats.title: Router `_validateDecimals` Underflow Blocks Valid Pool Creation
threats.hypothesis_description: The Router's `_validateDecimals` function uses unsigned subtraction `quoteDecimals - baseDecimals`. If the base token has more decimals than the quote token (e.g., WETH with 18 and USDC with 6), this will underflow and revert, preventing the creation of or liquidity addition to perfectly valid and common pool configurations.
threats.investigation_rationale: This code defect directly breaks core protocol functionality for standard token pairs, forcing users to bypass the Router or swap token ordering, which alters the AMM pricing semantics.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Unsigned subtraction `quoteDecimals - baseDecimals > MAX_BASE_QUOTE_DECIMALS_DIFFERENCE` without checking if base > quote

preconditions: Base token decimals > Quote token decimals

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Router reverts on valid token pairs like WETH/USDC

operational_questions: Are there any deployed pools where base decimals > quote decimals?
How does the frontend handle this revert?

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: User attempts to create a WETH/USDC pool via Router
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Transaction reverts with arithmetic underflow panic
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: N/A - Denial of Service is immediate

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Inability to use Router for common token pairs
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Pool creation and liquidity addition via Router

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed Router transactions with panic code 0x11
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users calling Factory directly instead of Router

threats.title: LockingMultiRewards Reward Claim DoS via Single Token Failure
threats.hypothesis_description: `LockingMultiRewards._getRewards` iterates over all `rewardTokens` and attempts to transfer them if the lock is expired. If any single reward token becomes non-transferable (e.g., a paused centralized stablecoin or a token that blacklists the user), the entire `getRewards` transaction will revert. Because there is no mechanism to remove a reward token or claim tokens individually, all accumulated rewards for that user become permanently locked.
threats.investigation_rationale: Reward distribution is a core value proposition. A single failing dependency (paused token) breaking the entire claim loop is a severe operational fragility.
deployment_assumptions: Owner adds a reward token that can be paused, blacklisted, or upgraded to revert on transfer

proof_of_signal.pattern: Unbounded loop over `rewardTokens` calling `safeTransfer` without try/catch or skip mechanism

preconditions: At least one reward token reverts on transfer to the user
User has an expired lock with non-zero rewards

initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Admin must have added the problematic token

operational_questions: What tokens are currently configured as rewards?
Do any of them have pause or blacklist functionality?
validation_requirements: Simulate a paused reward token and attempt to call `getRewards`

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Reward token (e.g., USDC) is paused globally or user is blacklisted
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Transaction reverts on the paused token's `safeTransfer`
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: User is unable to claim any other valid reward tokens

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users permanently unable to claim yield
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires contract upgrade as tokens cannot be removed

threats.title: Factory Pool Creation Salt Collision via `tx.origin`
threats.hypothesis_description: `Factory.create` uses `tx.origin` instead of `msg.sender` to compute the `CREATE2` salt. If a smart contract (like a DAO or an aggregator) deploys a pool, the pool is tied to the EOA that initiated the transaction. If that same EOA later attempts to deploy another pool with the same parameters (perhaps through a different protocol), the deployment will fail due to a salt collision. This also breaks on-chain attribution for protocols creating pools.
threats.investigation_rationale: Using `tx.origin` for state attribution breaks composability and can cause unexpected deployment failures for power users or relayers.
preconditions: Pool created via an intermediary contract
Same EOA attempts to create an identical pool later

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Attribution of pool ownership to EOA instead of calling contract

operational_questions: Are third-party protocols expected to deploy pools?
Does the frontend use relayers or smart contract wallets?
validation_requirements: Deploy pool via contract, then deploy identical pool from same EOA directly

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: User executes DAO proposal to create a pool via Router
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Pool is created with salt tied to the user's EOA
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: User later tries to create the same pool directly for testing
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Transaction reverts due to CREATE2 collision

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Failed pool deployments, broken indexing for factory events

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Pools showing EOA creators instead of expected contract creators

threats.title: MagicLP `correctRState` Arbitrage Sandwich
threats.hypothesis_description: The `correctRState` function is permissionless and resets the pricing curve state (`_RState_ = ONE`, targets = reserves) when reserves fall below targets. Because normal trading and fee extraction naturally cause reserves to drift below targets over time, an attacker can monitor for this condition, call `correctRState` to instantly shift the AMM price, and immediately execute a swap against the newly favorable curve to extract value from LPs.
threats.investigation_rationale: Permissionless state corrections that alter pricing curves are prime targets for MEV extraction, effectively taxing LPs.
preconditions: Reserves have drifted below targets due to accumulated fees

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Pricing curve shift triggered by unprivileged caller

operational_questions: How much value can be extracted per `correctRState` call?
Is the drift fast enough to make this profitable after gas?
validation_requirements: Simulate high volume trading, call `correctRState`, and measure arbitrage profit

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Execute swap at the newly reset `RState.ONE` price

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Continuous small value leakage from LPs

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: `correctRState` called in same transaction/bundle as swaps

threats.title: Fee Rate Evasion via `tx.origin` in Smart Contract Wallets
threats.hypothesis_description: `MagicLP` uses `tx.origin` instead of `msg.sender` when querying the `FeeRateModel`. If the protocol implements user-specific fee discounts (e.g., based on token holdings), a user trading through a smart contract wallet or an intent relayer will receive the fee rate of the relayer EOA, not their own. Conversely, an attacker could route their trades through a relayer known to have a favorable fee tier to evade fees.
threats.investigation_rationale: Using `tx.origin` for access control or fee tiering breaks account abstraction and allows privilege spoofing.
deployment_assumptions: FeeRateModel implementation provides variable rates based on the trader address
threats.config_evidence: FeeRateModelImpl.sol currently returns a flat rate, but the interface `getFeeRate(address trader, uint256 lpFeeRate)` is designed for variable rates.
preconditions: FeeRateModel upgraded to offer tiered fees
User uses a smart contract wallet or relayer

initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Admin must deploy a tiered FeeRateModel
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Always active if tiered model exists

operational_questions: Will a tiered fee model be deployed?
How does the protocol support Account Abstraction (ERC-4337)?
validation_requirements: Deploy tiered fee model, execute swap via relayer, verify wrong fee applied

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify an EOA with a high fee discount
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Construct a transaction that routes through that EOA (if it acts as a relayer) or trick the EOA into signing a meta-tx
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Execute swap, receiving the EOA's discount instead of the attacker's rate

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Loss of protocol fee revenue, broken UX for smart contract wallets

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Swaps originating from known relayers getting unexpected fee tiers

threats.title: MagicLP `buyShares` Permanent Bricking on Zero Reserve
threats.hypothesis_description: If either `_BASE_RESERVE_` or `_QUOTE_RESERVE_` reaches exactly 0 while `totalSupply() > 0`, the `buyShares` function will fail to execute either minting branch and will attempt to `_mint(to, 0)`, which reverts with `ErrMintAmountNotEnough`. This permanently bricks the pool from receiving new liquidity.
threats.investigation_rationale: Edge cases in state machine logic that lead to permanent dead-ends represent significant operational fragility.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing fallback branch: `else if (baseReserve > 0 && quoteReserve > 0)` leaves `shares = 0` if one reserve is 0

preconditions: One of the reserves is exactly 0 (e.g., via `setParameters` extraction)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Incomplete conditional coverage in share minting logic
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Admin action likely required to reach 0 reserve

operational_questions: Can a reserve reach 0 through normal trading rounding?
Is `setParameters` expected to be used to drain reserves?
validation_requirements: Set reserve to 0 via `setParameters`, attempt `buyShares`, verify revert

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Admin calls `setParameters` to extract all base tokens
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Users attempt to add liquidity via `buyShares`
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Transactions revert, pool is bricked for deposits

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Admin must call `setParameters` again to restore >0 reserve

threats.title: Reward Insolvency via Fee-on-Transfer Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: `LockingMultiRewards.notifyRewardAmount` uses the specified `amount` parameter to calculate the `rewardRate`, but uses `safeTransferFrom` to pull the tokens. If the reward token has a fee-on-transfer mechanism, the contract will receive less than `amount`, but will distribute rewards as if it received the full `amount`. This will lead to contract insolvency, causing later users' claim transactions to revert due to insufficient balance.
threats.investigation_rationale: Accounting mismatches between internal state and actual token balances inevitably lead to insolvency and locked user funds.
deployment_assumptions: Owner adds a fee-on-transfer token to `rewardTokens`
threats.config_evidence: No balance check is performed after `safeTransferFrom` in `notifyRewardAmount`.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `rewardToken.safeTransferFrom(..., amount);` followed by `rewardRate = amount / ...;` without balance delta check

preconditions: Reward token implements a transfer fee

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Trusting input `amount` over actual transferred balance
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Admin must configure a FoT token
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: End of epoch when contract runs out of tokens

operational_questions: Are there any plans to support FoT tokens as rewards?
Does the protocol monitor contract solvency?
validation_requirements: Deploy FoT token, notify rewards, simulate claims until insolvency

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Contract receives 900 tokens due to 10% transfer fee
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Contract distributes 1000 tokens over the epoch
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Last 10% of users to claim rewards face reverts due to empty contract balance

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users permanently unable to claim earned rewards
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Admin must manually transfer missing tokens to contract

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Reverting `getRewards` due to ERC20 transfer failure
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Contract reward balance < expected pending rewards

threats.title: ETH Pool Creation Reverts on Strict WETH Implementations
threats.hypothesis_description: `Router.createPoolETH` calls `weth.safeTransferFrom(address(this), clone, msg.value)` without first approving the Router to spend its own WETH. While standard WETH9 bypasses the allowance check when `src == msg.sender`, stricter WETH implementations or L2 native equivalents might enforce the allowance check universally, causing ETH pool creation to always revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: Relying on non-standard ERC20 behavior (allowance bypass for self-transfers) creates cross-chain deployment fragility.
deployment_assumptions: Deployed on a chain where the WETH equivalent strictly enforces allowances for self-transfers
threats.config_evidence: Blast.s.sol uses Blast's native WETH equivalent.
preconditions: WETH implementation requires allowance even when `src == msg.sender`

operational_questions: Does Blast's WETH implementation exactly mirror WETH9's allowance bypass?
Are there plans to deploy on chains with strict WETH?

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Transaction reverts due to insufficient allowance

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Inability to create ETH pools via Router

threats.title: Admin Rug Pull via `setParameters`
threats.hypothesis_description: The `setParameters` function in `MagicLP` allows the `implementationOwner` to specify arbitrary `baseOutAmount` and `quoteOutAmount` to be transferred to an `assetTo` address. While it checks that reserves don't fall below `minBaseReserve` and `minQuoteReserve` (which are also provided by the admin), a compromised admin key can use this to drain all tokens from the pool, stealing LP funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: Absolute administrative control over user funds represents a single point of failure and a critical operational risk.
preconditions: Admin key is compromised or admin acts maliciously

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Direct extraction of pool reserves by admin

operational_questions: Is the implementation owner a multisig or timelock?
Why does `setParameters` need to extract tokens?
validation_requirements: Verify ownership structure in deployment scripts

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Tokens are transferred to attacker's address

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Large token transfers out of pool via `setParameters`

threats.title: Reward Token Drain via `recover`
threats.hypothesis_description: The `recover` function in `LockingMultiRewards` allows the owner to transfer any token out of the contract. While it includes a safeguard to prevent skimming the core `stakingToken` below the required `stakingTokenBalance`, it lacks any such safeguard for the `rewardTokens`. A compromised owner could drain all accumulated reward tokens, leaving the contract insolvent and unable to pay users their earned rewards.
threats.investigation_rationale: Missing safeguards on admin recovery functions can lead to accidental or malicious draining of user assets.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing balance check for `rewardTokens` in `recover` function

preconditions: Admin key is compromised or admin acts maliciously

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Unrestricted token recovery for non-staking tokens

operational_questions: Is the owner a multisig or timelock?
Are there off-chain monitoring systems for `recover` calls?
validation_requirements: Call `recover` on a reward token and verify it drains user rewards

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Reward tokens are transferred to attacker

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Loss of all pending user rewards
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires admin to refund the contract

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: `recover` called with a reward token address
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Sudden drop in contract token balances
